topic: Exception Handling Strategies in Distributed Systems

questions.question: What is the primary cause of this issue?
options: A) Checked exceptions like `PaymentException` cannot trigger a rollback in Spring unless the class implements `Serializable`.

B) Spring's `@Transactional` uses proxy-based AOP. Since `executePaymentGatewayCall` is called from within the same class (`processOrder`), the call bypasses the Spring proxy and its transactional interceptor entirely.
C) The `orderRepository.save()` method operates on a separate JVM thread pool, isolating it from the transaction boundary of the service.
D) Class-level transaction managers default to `Propagation.NEVER` when encountering custom checked exceptions.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Spring's `@Transactional` uses proxy-based AOP. Since `executePaymentGatewayCall` is called from within the same class (`processOrder`), the call bypasses the Spring proxy and its transactional interceptor entirely.
questions.explanation: Spring's declarative transaction management (`@Transactional`) relies heavily on Spring AOP proxies (either JDK dynamic proxies or CGLIB class-based proxies). When an external client calls a method on a Spring-managed bean, the call goes through the proxy, which initiates the transaction. However, when a method within the class (`processOrder`) calls another method within the same class (`executePaymentGatewayCall`), it performs a local JVM 'this' call. This local invocation bypasses the proxy entirely, meaning the `@Transactional` annotation on `executePaymentGatewayCall` is completely ignored, and no transaction is started or rolled back.

questions.question: Why is the `traceId` missing from the MDC (Mapped Diagnostic Context) logs inside the asynchronous task and exception handler?
options: A) `CompletableFuture` executes callbacks in a separate JVM process where standard thread memory is not addressable.

B) MDC is backed by a `ThreadLocal` map. When executing tasks asynchronously using a thread pool like `customExecutor`, the worker threads do not automatically inherit the parent thread's `ThreadLocal` storage.
C) The `gatewayService` triggers garbage collection sweeps that flush the thread context to conserve heap memory.
D) `exceptionally` executes exclusively in the main thread which automatically purges all active MDC contexts for security.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) MDC is backed by a `ThreadLocal` map. When executing tasks asynchronously using a thread pool like `customExecutor`, the worker threads do not automatically inherit the parent thread's `ThreadLocal` storage.
questions.explanation: Mapped Diagnostic Context (MDC) is fundamentally built upon `ThreadLocal` variables in Java. Because `CompletableFuture.supplyAsync` schedules the task to run on a worker thread from `customExecutor`, the executing thread has an entirely different context map. To pass trace contexts across asynchronous boundaries, you must configure a custom thread pool executor or a Spring `TaskDecorator` that manually copies the MDC map from the submitting thread to the worker thread before execution.

questions.question: Why does updating the database status in the catch block fail?
options: A) The physical database connection is physically closed by the JDBC driver the moment any exception is thrown.

B) The unchecked `InventoryException` in `inventoryService.reserveInventory` propagates up, causing the transaction manager to mark the logical and physical transaction as 'rollback-only'. Any subsequent SQL operations on that transaction are rejected.
C) Spring's `@Transactional` requires all compensating methods to be annotated with `@Compensate` to allow emergency writes to rollback-only pools.
D) Hibernate automatically locks all repository beans when a runtime exception is caught, suspending the current JVM thread.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) The unchecked `InventoryException` in `inventoryService.reserveInventory` propagates up, causing the transaction manager to mark the logical and physical transaction as 'rollback-only'. Any subsequent SQL operations on that transaction are rejected.
questions.explanation: With `Propagation.REQUIRED` (the default propagation), `inventoryService.reserveInventory` participates in the outer transaction started by `processSaga`. When an unchecked exception (`RuntimeException` / `InventoryException`) propagates out of `reserveInventory`, the Spring transaction interceptor intercepts it and flags the transaction as 'rollback-only'. Even though the caller `processSaga` catches the exception in a `try-catch` block to handle compensation, the physical database transaction is already doomed. Any subsequent attempt to execute database writes (like `sagaRepository.updateStatus`) on the same 'rollback-only' transaction is illegal and will throw an `UnexpectedRollbackException` or transaction-inactive exception.
